sectionName: Chemical and Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
A physics lab investigates image formation by thin lenses. In Experiment 1, a converging lens with focal length f = 10 cm is mounted on an optical bench. An illuminated object (height hₒ = 3.0 cm) is placed at various distances from the lens, and the resulting image is captured on a movable screen. When the object is placed at dₒ = 25 cm, a sharp image forms on the screen at distance dᵢ from the lens, as shown in the ray diagram (Figure 1). The thin lens equation 1/f = 1/dₒ + 1/dᵢ and the magnification equation m = −dᵢ/dₒ = hᵢ/hₒ are used throughout.

In Experiment 2, the same lens is used to correct the vision of a patient with hyperopia (farsightedness). The patient's near point is 50 cm instead of the normal 25 cm. The lab also tests a diverging lens (f = −15 cm) for a myopic patient whose far point is 200 cm.
questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Ray diagram for a converging lens (f = 10 cm) with object at dₒ = 25 cm. The image forms at dᵢ ≈ 16.7 cm on the opposite side, real and inverted.
questions.stem: Based on Figure 1, the image formed when dₒ = 25 cm is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: real, inverted, and reduced in size

choices.label: D
choices.text: virtual, inverted, and reduced in size

explanations.A: Correct. Using the thin lens equation: 1/dᵢ = 1/f − 1/dₒ = 1/10 − 1/25 = (5−2)/50 = 3/50, so dᵢ = 16.7 cm (positive, confirming a real image on the opposite side). Magnification m = −16.7/25 = −0.67. The negative sign indicates inversion, and |m| < 1 indicates the image is smaller than the object: hᵢ = 0.67 × 3.0 = 2.0 cm. When dₒ > 2f, a converging lens always produces a real, inverted, reduced image.
explanations.B: A real upright image cannot be formed by a single thin lens. For a converging lens, real images (formed on the opposite side of the lens) are always inverted. Upright images from converging lenses are virtual and occur only when the object is between the lens and the focal point (dₒ < f).
explanations.C: Virtual, upright, enlarged images form when the object is placed between the focal point and the lens (dₒ < f), producing a magnifying glass effect. Here dₒ = 25 cm > f = 10 cm, so the object is well beyond the focal point. The positive dᵢ from the thin lens equation confirms a real, not virtual, image.
explanations.D: Virtual images from a single converging lens are always upright, not inverted. A virtual image forms when dᵢ is negative (image on the same side as the object). With dₒ = 25 cm and f = 10 cm, dᵢ = +16.7 cm (positive), indicating a real image. Virtual inverted images do not occur with simple thin lenses.

questions.stem: If the object is moved to dₒ = 15 cm from the same lens, what is the image distance and magnification?

explanations.A: Correct. 1/dᵢ = 1/f − 1/dₒ = 1/10 − 1/15 = (3−2)/30 = 1/30, so dᵢ = 30 cm. Magnification m = −dᵢ/dₒ = −30/15 = −2.0. The image is real (positive dᵢ), inverted (negative m), and enlarged (|m| = 2.0). When the object is between f and 2f (10 < 15 < 20), the image forms beyond 2f on the other side and is enlarged — this is the configuration used in projectors.
explanations.B: This answer applies the thin lens equation incorrectly, possibly by adding 1/dₒ instead of subtracting, or by computing 1/dᵢ = 1/15 − 1/10 = −1/30 and then taking the absolute value. The correct computation gives dᵢ = 30 cm, not 6 cm.
explanations.C: A negative dᵢ would indicate a virtual image on the same side as the object. With dₒ = 15 cm > f = 10 cm, the object is beyond the focal point, guaranteeing a real image with positive dᵢ. Additionally, real images from converging lenses are inverted (negative m), not upright (positive m).
explanations.D: The dᵢ = 30 cm is correct, but the magnification sign is wrong. m = −dᵢ/dₒ = −30/15 = −2.0, not +2.0. The negative sign is essential — it indicates the image is inverted. An upright image (positive m) would require dₒ < f, placing the object inside the focal length.

questions.stem: The object is now placed at dₒ = 10 cm, exactly at the focal point. What happens to the image?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The image forms at dᵢ = 20 cm with unit magnification

choices.label: B
choices.text: A virtual image forms at the focal point on the same side as the object

choices.label: C
choices.text: No image forms because the refracted rays emerge parallel and never converge

choices.label: D
choices.text: The image forms at infinity and is infinitely small

explanations.A: If dₒ = 10 cm and f = 10 cm: 1/dᵢ = 1/10 − 1/10 = 0, so dᵢ → ∞. The image does not form at 20 cm. The unit magnification case (m = −1) occurs when dₒ = 2f = 20 cm, where the image forms at dᵢ = 20 cm with the same size as the object.
explanations.B: A virtual image on the object side occurs when dₒ < f, giving a negative dᵢ. At dₒ = f exactly, the rays are parallel (neither converging nor diverging), so no image — real or virtual — forms at any finite location. The image is said to form 'at infinity.'
explanations.C: Correct. When dₒ = f, the thin lens equation gives 1/dᵢ = 1/f − 1/f = 0, so dᵢ = ∞. Rays from each point on the object emerge from the lens parallel to each other (collimated beam), never converging to form an image. This principle is used in collimating systems and spotlights: placing a light source at the focal point produces a parallel beam. No real or virtual image can be captured on a screen at any finite distance.
explanations.D: While dᵢ → ∞ is correct, the image is not 'infinitely small.' The concept of an image at infinity means the refracted rays are parallel and no image is formed at any accessible distance. The angular size of the object as seen through the lens approaches a finite value, which is the basis for angular magnification in optical instruments.

questions.topic: Object at focal point — no image formation

questions.stem: Two thin lenses with focal lengths f₁ = +20 cm and f₂ = +5 cm are placed in contact. What is the combined focal length of this lens system?

explanations.A: Simply adding focal lengths (20 + 5 = 25) is incorrect. Lens powers (diopters), not focal lengths, are additive for thin lenses in contact. The correct relationship is 1/f_total = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂.
explanations.B: 100 cm would result from multiplying the focal lengths (20 × 5 = 100). The correct formula requires adding reciprocals: 1/f = 1/20 + 1/5. Multiplication of focal lengths has no physical basis in thin lens optics.
explanations.C: 12.5 cm would result from averaging the two focal lengths ((20 + 5)/2 = 12.5). Focal lengths do not average; their reciprocals add. The correct approach uses the power addition formula: 1/f = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂.
explanations.D: Correct. For thin lenses in contact, powers add: P_total = P₁ + P₂, or equivalently 1/f_total = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂ = 1/20 + 1/5 = 1/20 + 4/20 = 5/20 = 1/4, so f_total = 4 cm. This combination has a power of 25 diopters (P = 1/0.04 m). The combined system is more converging than either lens alone, and the result is dominated by the stronger lens (f₂ = 5 cm, P₂ = 20 D).

questions.topic: Thin lenses in contact — power addition

questions.stem: For the hyperopic patient whose near point is 50 cm, what power corrective lens is needed to allow reading at the normal 25 cm?

explanations.A: A diverging lens (negative power) would make the near point even farther away, worsening hyperopia. Diverging lenses are used to correct myopia, where the far point is too close. Hyperopia requires a converging (positive) lens to bring the near point closer.
explanations.B: Correct. The corrective lens must take an object at 25 cm and form a virtual image at the patient's near point of 50 cm, where the eye can focus. Using the thin lens equation with dₒ = 25 cm and dᵢ = −50 cm (virtual image on same side as object): 1/f = 1/25 + 1/(−50) = 2/50 − 1/50 = 1/50 cm. So f = 50 cm = 0.50 m, giving P = 1/0.50 = +2.0 D. The positive sign confirms a converging lens, appropriate for hyperopia.
explanations.C: +4.0 D corresponds to f = 25 cm. This would form an image at infinity for an object at 25 cm (1/∞ = 1/25 − 1/25 = 0), which is overcorrection. The patient needs the image at 50 cm, not infinity. A +4.0 D lens would be appropriate only if the near point were at infinity (extreme hyperopia).
explanations.D: A −4.0 D diverging lens would make the near point much worse for a hyperopic patient. Negative lenses diverge light rays, pushing the image farther from the eye. This lens would be used for a myopic patient with a far point of 25 cm.

questions.passage:
In a separate experiment, the lab studies reflection and image formation by curved mirrors. A concave mirror with radius of curvature R = 30 cm (focal length f = R/2 = 15 cm) is used to examine image properties at different object distances. The mirror equation 1/f = 1/dₒ + 1/dᵢ applies, with the sign convention that distances are positive in front of the mirror (real) and negative behind it (virtual).

The lab also examines total internal reflection in optical fibers. A fiber optic cable has a glass core with refractive index n₁ = 1.50 and a cladding with n₂ = 1.40. Light enters the fiber and undergoes total internal reflection at the core-cladding interface when the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle θc. Snell's law (n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂) governs refraction at interfaces.
questions.stem: An object is placed 10 cm in front of the concave mirror (dₒ = 10 cm, between f and the mirror). What type of image forms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Real, inverted, enlarged — formed in front of the mirror

choices.label: B
choices.text: Real, upright, reduced — formed at the focal point

choices.label: C
choices.text: Virtual, upright, enlarged — formed behind the mirror

choices.label: D
choices.text: No image forms because the object is inside the focal length

explanations.A: Real, inverted images from concave mirrors form when dₒ > f. Here dₒ = 10 cm < f = 15 cm, so the object is inside the focal length. 1/dᵢ = 1/15 − 1/10 = (2−3)/30 = −1/30, giving dᵢ = −30 cm. The negative dᵢ indicates a virtual image behind the mirror, not a real image in front of it.
explanations.B: Real upright images cannot be formed by a single concave mirror. All real images from concave mirrors are inverted. Additionally, the object is inside the focal length, so no real image forms at all — the reflected rays diverge and must be traced backward to locate the virtual image behind the mirror.
explanations.C: Correct. With dₒ = 10 cm < f = 15 cm: 1/dᵢ = 1/15 − 1/10 = −1/30, so dᵢ = −30 cm. The negative sign indicates the image is behind the mirror (virtual). Magnification m = −(−30)/10 = +3.0 — positive (upright) and |m| = 3 (enlarged). This is the principle behind magnifying mirrors used in makeup and shaving: placing the face inside the focal length produces an enlarged, upright, virtual image.
explanations.D: An image always forms, regardless of object position relative to the focal length. When dₒ < f for a concave mirror, reflected rays diverge after reflection but appear to originate from a point behind the mirror, producing a virtual image. The thin lens/mirror equation always yields a solution; the sign of dᵢ indicates whether the image is real or virtual.

questions.topic: Concave mirror image formation inside focal length

questions.stem: What is the critical angle for total internal reflection in the optical fiber described?

explanations.A: The formula uses the correct ratio, but the numerical value is wrong. sin⁻¹(1.40/1.50) = sin⁻¹(0.933) ≈ 69°, not 21°. An angle near 21° would come from using cos⁻¹(0.933), which is not the critical-angle relationship from Snell's law.
explanations.B: This incorrectly uses n₁/n₂ = 1.50/1.40 = 1.071 in the sine function. Since sin cannot exceed 1, sin⁻¹(1.071) is undefined — no real angle satisfies this equation. The critical angle formula requires the ratio of the less-dense to the more-dense medium: sin θc = n₂/n₁ where n₂ < n₁.
explanations.C: Correct. For total internal reflection from the higher-index core (n₁ = 1.50) into the lower-index cladding (n₂ = 1.40), the critical angle satisfies sin θc = n₂/n₁. Thus θc = sin⁻¹(1.40/1.50) = sin⁻¹(0.933) ≈ 69°. Light striking the boundary at angles greater than this angle, measured from the normal, undergoes total internal reflection.
explanations.D: The critical angle uses the sine function from Snell's law, not cosine. At the critical angle, the refracted ray travels along the interface (θ₂ = 90°), so n₁ sin θc = n₂ sin 90° = n₂, giving sin θc = n₂/n₁. Using cosine has no basis in Snell's law.

questions.topic: Critical angle and total internal reflection

questions.stem: A convex mirror with focal length f = −20 cm always produces images that are:

choices.label: A
choices.text: virtual, inverted, and reduced — for all object distances

choices.label: B
choices.text: real, inverted, and enlarged — when the object is beyond the center of curvature

choices.label: C
choices.text: either real or virtual depending on whether the object is inside or outside the focal length

choices.label: D
choices.text: virtual, upright, and reduced — regardless of object distance

explanations.A: Convex mirrors produce upright images, not inverted. The magnification is always positive (m > 0) for convex mirrors because dᵢ is negative. A virtual inverted image would require positive m with a negative interpretation, which is geometrically impossible for diverging mirrors.
explanations.B: Convex mirrors never produce real images. All reflected rays diverge after reflection from a convex surface, so they can never converge to form a real image. Real, inverted images are produced by concave mirrors when dₒ > f.
explanations.C: This describes concave mirrors, which produce real images when dₒ > f and virtual images when dₒ < f. Convex mirrors have no such transition because f is behind the mirror (negative) — no real object distance can satisfy dₒ < |f| in the sense needed for a real image.
explanations.D: Correct. A convex (diverging) mirror always produces virtual, upright, reduced images regardless of object position. Since f is negative, 1/dᵢ = 1/f − 1/dₒ is always negative (because 1/f < 0 and −1/dₒ < 0), giving dᵢ < 0 (virtual). The magnification m = −dᵢ/dₒ is always positive (upright) and |m| < 1 (reduced) because |dᵢ| < dₒ. This property makes convex mirrors useful as wide-angle security mirrors and vehicle side mirrors — the image is always visible and provides a wide field of view.

questions.stem: Light traveling through the glass core (n = 1.50) of the optical fiber strikes the core-cladding interface at 75° from the normal. Which of the following occurs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The light refracts into the cladding at an angle greater than 75°

choices.label: B
choices.text: The light undergoes total internal reflection because 75° exceeds the critical angle of approximately 69°

choices.label: C
choices.text: The light is absorbed by the cladding material

choices.label: D
choices.text: The light refracts into the cladding at an angle less than 75°

explanations.A: Refraction into the cladding would occur if the incident angle were below the critical angle (~69°). At 75° > 69°, no refracted ray enters the cladding — all light is reflected back into the core. When refraction does occur at subcritical angles, the refracted angle is indeed larger than the incident angle (since n₁ > n₂), but at 75° the light never enters the cladding.
explanations.B: Correct. The critical angle is θc = sin⁻¹(n₂/n₁) = sin⁻¹(1.40/1.50) = sin⁻¹(0.933) ≈ 69°. Since the angle of incidence (75°) exceeds θc, total internal reflection occurs — all light reflects back into the glass core with no transmission into the cladding. This is the fundamental operating principle of fiber optics: light signals propagate through the core via repeated total internal reflections, minimizing signal loss over long distances.
explanations.C: Optical fibers are specifically designed so that the cladding does not absorb light at the operating wavelengths. Signal loss in fiber optics comes from scattering and absorption in the core, not cladding absorption. At 75° > θc, the light never enters the cladding at all due to total internal reflection.
explanations.D: When light passes from a denser medium (higher n) to a less dense medium (lower n), the refracted angle is always larger than the incident angle, not smaller. At subcritical angles, n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂ with n₁ > n₂ gives θ₂ > θ₁. But at 75° > θc, no refraction occurs — the light is totally internally reflected.

questions.topic: Total internal reflection in fiber optics

questions.stem: For the myopic patient with a far point of 200 cm, what power diverging lens corrects vision to see distant objects clearly?

explanations.A: −2.0 D corresponds to f = −50 cm. This lens would form a virtual image of a distant object at 50 cm, which would overcorrect this patient. The patient's far point is 200 cm, so the virtual image should form at 200 cm, not 50 cm. A −2.0 D lens is appropriate for a far point of 50 cm.
explanations.B: +0.50 D is a converging lens (f = +200 cm). Converging lenses are used for hyperopia, not myopia. A converging lens would focus distant light even more strongly, worsening the myopic patient's condition by bringing the image to a point in front of the retina.
explanations.C: Correct. The lens must form a virtual image of distant objects (dₒ = ∞) at the patient's far point (dᵢ = −200 cm = −2.0 m). Using 1/f = 1/dₒ + 1/dᵢ = 1/∞ + 1/(−200) = −1/200 cm = −0.005 cm⁻¹. So f = −200 cm = −2.0 m, giving P = 1/(−2.0) = −0.50 D. The negative power confirms a diverging lens, which is appropriate for myopia — it diverges incoming parallel rays so they appear to come from the far point.
explanations.D: +2.0 D is a converging lens with f = +50 cm, appropriate for correcting significant hyperopia. Using a converging lens for a myopic patient would dramatically worsen their distance vision by adding even more converging power to an already overpowered optical system.
